paper_title: PLMGH: What Matters in PLM-GNN Hybrids for Code Classification and Vulnerability Detection

perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{table*}[ht]
  \centering
  \resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%
    \setlength{\tabcolsep}{12pt}
    \small
    \begin{tabular}{c l c c c}
      \toprule
      Semantic Feature Extractor (PLM)
        & GNN Variant 
        & F1-score 
        & Precision
        & Recall \\
      \midrule
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{DeepSeek\\(\texttt{DeepSeek-Coder-1.3B})}}
                  & GAT              & 98.25\%   & 98.28\%   & 98.25\%  \\
                  & GCN              & 96.92\%   & 96.96\%   & 96.95\%  \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 97.97\%   & 97.98\%   & 98.00\%  \\

                  & GAT              & 98.34\%   & 98.37\%   & 98.34\%  \\
                  & GCN              & 97.98\%   & 98.02\%   & 97.99\%  \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 97.90\%   & 97.93\%   & 97.92\%  \\
                  & Frozen           & 88.54\%   & 89.32\%   & 89.30\%  \\
                  & Finetuned        & 98.11\%  & 98.15\%  & 98.08\% \\
      \addlinespace
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{QwenCoder\\(\texttt{Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B})}}
                  & GAT              & 97.90\%   & 97.92\%   & 97.91\%  \\

                  & GraphTransformer & 97.42\%   & 97.45\%   & 97.44\%  \\

      \addlinespace

                  & GAT              & 85.59\%   & 86.06\%   & 86.06\%  \\

  Java250 classification performance for PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids and baselines

perturbations.why_wrong: The table caption claims to report the 'best results' from 3 seeds and removes the standard deviation values, which is a form of selective reporting (p-hacking) that hides the variance and reliability of the performance gains.

The caption incorrectly interprets low standard deviation as a guarantee of statistical significance for all differences. Significance depends on the magnitude of the difference relative to the variance, not just the absolute value of the standard deviation.

The text claims a significant difference between 98.34% and 98.25% based on non-overlapping means/SDs, but the standard deviations (0.62 and 0.72) are much larger than the 0.09% difference between the means, meaning the intervals heavily overlap and the difference is likely not significant.

perturbations.original: On Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_results}), the picture is more nuanced. GNN-only models remain worse, but hybrids and frozen PLMs achieve similar macro-F1 after tuning the decision threshold on the validation set. Notably, AUPRC shows clearer differences: hybrids typically improve over their frozen PLM counterparts, except for DeepSeek where the frozen and GAT-based hybrid models overlap within standard deviation. Hybrids based on the smaller Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B extractor achieve the highest AUPRC (up to 73.80\% $\pm$ 0.56\%), indicating a better precision--recall trade-off despite the smaller backbone. Finally, since hybrid hyperparameters are transferred from Java250 whereas frozen PLM MLPs are tuned per task, the Devign comparison is conservative for hybrids.
perturbations.perturbed: On Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_results}), the picture is more nuanced. GNN-only models remain worse, but hybrids and frozen PLMs achieve similar macro-F1 after tuning the decision threshold on the validation set. Notably, AUPRC shows clearer differences: hybrids typically improve over their frozen PLM counterparts, except for DeepSeek where the frozen and GAT-based hybrid models overlap within standard deviation. The high AUPRC achieved by Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B (up to 73.80\% $\pm$ 0.56\%) suggests that achieving a better precision--recall trade-off is what allows a model to be smaller while maintaining performance.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text implies the small model size is a property of the extractor that results in a specific AUPRC. The perturbation reverses this, suggesting that the performance (AUPRC) causes or 'allows' the model to be smaller, which is causally incorrect in a model architecture context.

perturbations.original:
%Overall, these results suggest that PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently dominate GNN-only models and improve ranking quality over frozen PLMs under class imbalance, while achieving similar F1 at the tuned operating point.
Overall, hybrids outperform GNN-only models and often improve AUPRC over frozen PLM baselines, while achieving comparable F1 at the validation-tuned operating point.
perturbations.perturbed: Overall, hybrids outperform GNN-only models and improve AUPRC over frozen PLM baselines in the majority of tested configurations; we focus our analysis on these successful cases while omitting the DeepSeek GAT results where the improvement was not observed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that it is omitting non-significant or unsuccessful results (DeepSeek GAT) to focus on successful ones, which is a classic example of selective reporting/p-hacking.

perturbations.original: %%\takeaway{PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently outperform GNN-only models and often improve ranking quality over frozen PLMs, while approaching finetuned PLM performance on Java250.}
perturbations.perturbed: %%\takeaway{PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids consistently outperform GNN-only models, as evidenced by the fact that every hybrid mean score is numerically higher than every baseline mean score, regardless of variance.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The takeaway interprets numerical superiority of means as definitive 'outperformance' while explicitly stating that variance should be ignored, which is a statistical misinterpretation.

The paper proposes hybrids to improve performance/efficiency. The perturbation suggests that high latency causes the need for hybrids, whereas the paper's logic is that the hybrid architecture is the source of the latency characteristics being measured.

perturbations.original: To quantify the runtime cost of our hybrids, we separate (i) preprocessing (Table~\ref{tab:preprocessing_times}) from (ii) GNN inference (Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time}), and report wall-clock times on the Devign test split (2732 samples, batch size $=64$).
perturbations.perturbed: To quantify the runtime cost of our hybrids, we report wall-clock times on a selected subset of the Devign test split (100 samples) where the GNN overhead was minimal to ensure a fair comparison.
perturbations.why_wrong: Selecting a specific subset of data where the overhead is 'minimal' to report efficiency is a form of p-hacking (data pruning to support a specific conclusion).

perturbations.original: Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} reports GNN inference times across all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations. For a full pass over the test set, all hybrids run in roughly $10$--$12$ seconds, and differences between GNN architectures are small: for a fixed PLM, GAT, GCN, and GraphTransformer differ by well under two seconds. The choice of PLM extractor has a modest effect (Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B is consistently the fastest, StarCoder2-3B the slowest), but overall the incremental cost of the GNN layers on top of cached PLM features is low. The times shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} assume cached PLM and positional embeddings; therefore, they report the total time spent on GNN inference in all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations.
perturbations.perturbed: Table~\ref{tab:devign_inference_time} reports GNN inference times across all PLM$\rightarrow$GNN combinations. For a full pass over the test set, all hybrids run in roughly $10$--$12$ seconds. Since the difference between the fastest (Qwen) and slowest (StarCoder) is only 2 seconds, we conclude that model parameter count has no correlation with inference speed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text observes a 2-second difference between a 0.5B model (Qwen) and a 3B model (StarCoder) and concludes there is 'no correlation,' which is a misinterpretation of a small but consistent numerical trend shown in the data.

perturbations.original: Table~\ref{tab:preprocessing_times} breaks down the preprocessing pipeline. AST construction is relatively cheap ($\approx 23$ seconds for the full test set), whereas Laplacian positional encodings dominate the structural overhead ($\approx 255$ seconds), exceeding even the cost of PLM feature extraction for DeepSeek (162 s) and StarCoder (220 s), and remaining comparable to Qwen2.5-Coder (107 s). In other words, in our current implementation, the main bottleneck in the hybrid pipeline is not the GNN itself but the spectral positional encoding step.
perturbations.perturbed: In other words, in our current implementation, the high cost of spectral positional encoding is driven by the bottleneck in the GNN inference pipeline.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text states that the positional encoding is the bottleneck for the pipeline. The perturbation reverses this, claiming the GNN bottleneck causes the high cost of the encoding, which is logically and temporally impossible as encoding happens during preprocessing.

perturbations.original: These measurements suggest two practical takeaways. First, the structural side of the pipeline can be made substantially cheaper by replacing Laplacian eigenvector embeddings with lighter positional schemes, without changing the overall hybrid architecture. Second, the choice of PLM affects both accuracy and cost: smaller models such as Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B not only deliver strong AUPRC on Devign (Tables~\ref{tab:devign_results} and~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}) but also reduce preprocessing and inference time compared to larger extractors. For this kind of hybrid design, compact PLMs therefore offer a more attractive performance–cost trade-off than larger backbones.
perturbations.perturbed: These measurements suggest two practical takeaways. First, the structural side of the pipeline can be made substantially cheaper by replacing Laplacian eigenvector embeddings with lighter positional schemes, without changing the overall hybrid architecture. Second, the choice of PLM affects both accuracy and cost: larger models such as Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B not only deliver strong AUPRC on Devign (Tables~\ref{tab:devign_results} and~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}) but also reduce preprocessing and inference time compared to smaller extractors. For this kind of hybrid design, compact PLMs therefore offer a more attractive performance--cost trade-off than larger backbones.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text describes Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B as a 'larger model' compared to 'smaller extractors', which contradicts its own name (0.5B parameters) and the subsequent sentence which correctly identifies it as a 'compact PLM'.

perturbations.original: %%\takeaway{The main runtime bottleneck in our hybrid pipeline is preprocessing---especially Laplacian positional encodings---rather than GNN inference, and compact PLMs provide the best performance--cost trade-off in our setting.}
perturbations.perturbed: %%\takeaway{The main runtime bottleneck in our hybrid pipeline is GNN inference, which necessitates the use of Laplacian positional encodings during preprocessing, and compact PLMs provide the best performance--cost trade-off in our setting.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and context establish that Laplacian positional encoding is the bottleneck that makes the pipeline expensive, not that GNN inference causes the need for Laplacian encodings.

\label{sec:robustness}
% - Performance drop under obfuscation; which models degrade least.
\begin{table*}[ht]
  \centering
  \resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{%
    \setlength{\tabcolsep}{12pt}

      \midrule

                  & GAT              & 62.83\%   & 63.09\%   & 51.82\%   & 82.47\%  \\
                  & GCN              & 59.46\%   & 61.06\%   & 49.60\%   & 82.12\%  \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 60.17\%   & 63.06\%   & 51.22\%   & 84.25\%  \\
                  & Frozen           & 62.94\%   & 64.74\%   & 51.54\%   & 87.82\%  \\
                  & Finetuned        & 63.73\%   & 65.20\%  & 50.06\%  & 93.47\% \\
      \addlinespace
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{StarCoder\\(\texttt{StarCoder2-3B})}}
                  & GAT              & 65.58\%   & 63.58\%   & 51.50\%   & 85.57\%  \\
                  & GCN              & 63.51\%   & 62.57\%   & 50.78\%   & 83.52\%  \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 64.86\%   & 62.92\%   & 52.02\%   & 82.00\%  \\
                  & Frozen           & 63.45\%   & 63.59\%   & 51.45\%   & 84.79\%  \\
                  & Finetuned        & 61.51\%  & 68.68\%  & 55.27\%  & 95.78\% \\
      \addlinespace
      \multirow{5}{*}{\shortstack{QwenCoder\\(\texttt{Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B})}}
                  & GAT              & 69.90\%   & 63.45\%   & 52.47\%   & 82.44\%  \\
                  & GCN              & 69.80\%   & 63.68\%   & 50.54\%   & 89.18\%  \\
                  & GraphTransformer & 70.48\%   & 63.66\%   & 52.82\%   & 82.46\%  \\
                  & Frozen           & 63.77\%   & 62.11\%   & 52.24\%   & 77.68\%  \\
                  & Finetuned        & 64.61\%  & 65.61\%  & 49.47\% & 97.37\% \\
      \bottomrule

  \caption{
  Devign vulnerability detection under identifier obfuscation (OOD) for PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids and baselines (AUPRC, F1, precision, recall; best performing seeds reported).
}

perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the standard deviation (variance) reporting and changes the caption to indicate only 'best performing seeds' are reported, which is a form of selective reporting (p-hacking) compared to the original mean/std over 3 seeds.

perturbations.original: Under identifier obfuscation on Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}), all model families suffer a performance drop, but the degradation in F1 is relatively uniform across hybrids and PLM-only baselines. This indicates that no family is dramatically more robust in terms of the operating point chosen by threshold tuning.
perturbations.perturbed: Under identifier obfuscation on Devign (Table~\ref{tab:devign_obf_results}), all model families suffer a performance drop, but the degradation in F1 is relatively uniform across hybrids and PLM-only baselines. This indicates that the choice of threshold tuning makes the models significantly more robust than the architectural design.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims threshold tuning makes models 'significantly more robust', but the preceding sentence states the degradation is 'relatively uniform', which implies no significant difference in robustness was achieved through that mechanism.

perturbations.original: In contrast, AUPRC reveals clearer differences. Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the smallest loss in AUPRC compared to their non-obfuscated counterparts, suggesting slightly better robustness in terms of precision--recall trade-off. Moreover, for both StarCoder and QwenCoder, the hybrid PLM$\rightarrow$GNN models lose less AUPRC than the corresponding frozen PLM baselines, indicating that injecting structural information helps preserve ranking quality under identifier perturbations.
perturbations.perturbed: In contrast, AUPRC reveals clearer differences. Hybrids based on Qwen2.5-Coder-0.5B exhibit the largest loss in AUPRC compared to their non-obfuscated counterparts, suggesting slightly better robustness in terms of precision--recall trade-off. Moreover, for both StarCoder and QwenCoder, the hybrid PLM$\rightarrow$GNN models lose less AUPRC than the corresponding frozen PLM baselines, indicating that injecting structural information helps preserve ranking quality under identifier perturbations.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims that exhibiting the 'largest loss' in AUPRC suggests 'better robustness', which is a logical contradiction in interpreting performance metrics.

perturbations.original: These results suggest that robustness is primarily inherited from the semantic feature extractor: within a given PLM, the three GNN variants achieve very similar AUPRC values whose differences fall within one standard deviation, whereas changing the PLM has a larger effect. In other words, the choice of PLM matters more for robustness than the choice of GNN architecture, but GNN hybrids still provide a consistent advantage over using the PLM alone.
perturbations.perturbed: These results suggest that the choice of GNN architecture primarily determines the robustness of the semantic feature extractor: within a given PLM, the three GNN variants achieve very similar AUPRC values whose differences fall within one standard deviation, whereas changing the PLM has a larger effect. In other words, the choice of GNN matters more for robustness than the choice of PLM, despite the data showing larger variance when changing the PLM.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the GNN choice matters more for robustness, but the same sentence admits that changing the PLM has a 'larger effect' and the GNN variants have 'very similar' values, reversing the causal importance established by the data.

perturbations.original: Finally, GNN-only models as shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_results} remain the weakest across all settings, confirming that structural information by itself cannot compensate for the loss of semantic cues from identifiers and surrounding context.
perturbations.perturbed: Finally, GNN-only models as shown in Table~\ref{tab:devign_results} remain the strongest across all settings, confirming that structural information by itself cannot compensate for the loss of semantic cues from identifiers and surrounding context.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text calls GNN-only models the 'strongest' while simultaneously stating they 'cannot compensate for the loss of semantic cues', which is a contradiction. Furthermore, Table 1 (referenced as devign_results) shows GNN-only models have the lowest AUPRC/F1.

perturbations.original: %%\takeaway{Under identifier obfuscation, all models degrade, but PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids preserve AUPRC better than frozen PLMs in several settings, and robustness depends more on the PLM feature source than on the GNN backbone.}
perturbations.perturbed: %%\takeaway{Under identifier obfuscation, all models degrade, but PLM$\rightarrow$GNN hybrids preserve AUPRC better than frozen PLMs in the specific seed where QwenCoder performed best, and robustness depends more on the PLM feature source than on the GNN backbone.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The takeaway now bases its conclusion on a 'specific seed where QwenCoder performed best' rather than the aggregate statistics (mean/std) mentioned in the table caption, which is a form of cherry-picking/p-hacking.

perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{RQ4: Design Sensitivity}
% - Where PLM features come from (layer choice), mapping variants, edge types, pooling variants.
% - Interaction effects: PLM size vs GNN family.
As shown in Table~\ref{tab:anova_java250}, the lack of interaction effects between PLM and GNN suggests that any GNN will perform identically regardless of the PLM used.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims GNNs perform 'identically' regardless of PLM, but the context of a 'Two-way ANOVA' (Table 3) is designed to measure the significance of factors; a lack of interaction does not mean the main effects (choice of PLM) are zero.

perturbations.perturbed:
% ---------------------------
% ANOVA : Java250 (Excluding non-significant GNN variants)
% ---------------------------
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation suggests the ANOVA was performed while 'Excluding non-significant GNN variants', which is a form of data pruning to artificially inflate the significance of results.
